channel.title: Function-Call on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Function-Call on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] R Error — Argument Missing, With No Default Fix
item.description:
Argument Missing, With No Default — Fix

The error Error in function_name() : argument "X" is missing, with no default occurs when you call a function without providing a required argument that has no default value.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Forgetting to provide a required argument
my_func <- function(x, y) {
 return(x + y)
}
my_func() # Error: argument "x" is missing, with no default

# Cause 2: Named argument omitted
my_func <- function(data, column, default = NA) {
 return(data[[column]])
}
my_func(iris) # Error: argument "column" is missing, with no default

# Cause 3: Missing argument in function call
paste("hello", , "world") # Error: argument "sep" is missing

# Cause 4: Calling internal functions incorrectly
read.csv(file = "data.csv", header = TRUE, sep = ) # Error

How to Fix

Fix 1: Provide all required arguments

# Wrong
my_func <- function(x, y) {
 return(x + y)
}
result <- my_func(5)

# Correct
my_func <- function(x, y) {
 return(x + y)
}
result <- my_func(5, 3) # result: 8

Fix 2: Add default values to function parameters

# Wrong — no defaults
calculate <- function(a, b, c) {
 return(a + b + c)
}
calculate(1, 2) # Error: argument "c" is missing

# Correct — add defaults
calculate <- function(a, b, c = 0) {
 return(a + b + c)
}
calculate(1, 2) # Works: result is 3

Fix 3: Use missing() to check in function body

# Robust function with argument checking
safe_func <- function(x, y) {
 if (missing(x)) stop("x is required")
 if (missing(y)) stop("y is required")
 return(x + y)
}

Fix 4: Use … for optional arguments

# Flexible function
plot_custom <- function(x, ...) {
 plot(x, ...)
}
plot_custom(1:10, main = "My Plot", col = "blue")

Examples

# Example 1: Missing required argument
multiply <- function(a, b) {
 return(a * b)
}
multiply(5)
# Error in multiply(5) : argument "b" is missing, with no default

# Example 2: In paste function
paste("hello", , "world")
# Error in paste("hello", , "world") : argument "sep" is missing, with no default

# Example 3: In read.csv
read.csv()
# Error: argument "file" is missing, with no default

# Example 4: Custom function
greet <- function(name, greeting) {
 return(paste(greeting, name))
}
greet("Alice")
# Error in greet("Alice") : argument "greeting" is missing, with no default

Related Errors

  • unused-argument — argument not used
  • object-not-found — undefined variable
  • error-in-source — sourcing files

item.title: [Solution] R Error — Unused Argument Fix
item.description:
Unused Argument — Fix

The error Error in function_name(unused_arg = value) : unused argument (unused_arg = value) occurs when you pass a named argument to a function that does not accept it. The function signature does not include that parameter.

Common Causes

# Cause 1: Typo in argument name
data <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
mean(dat = data) # Error: unused argument (dat = data)

# Cause 2: Argument belongs to a different function
plot(1:10, col = "blue", lwd = 2)
# Works for plot(), but not for points() if called separately

# Cause 3: Using ggplot arguments in base R
plot(1:10, title = "My Plot") # Error: 'title' is not a plot() argument

# Cause 4: Passing extra arguments to simple functions
length(x = 1:10) # Error: unused argument (x = 1:10)

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check function documentation

# Wrong — 'colour' is not a plot argument in base R
plot(1:10, colour = "red")

# Correct — use 'col' instead
plot(1:10, col = "red")

Fix 2: Use … for flexible argument passing

# Wrong — sum() doesn't accept 'na.rm' as named
sum(1, 2, na.rm = TRUE)

# Correct — na.rm is a valid argument
sum(c(1, 2, NA), na.rm = TRUE)

Fix 3: Verify function signature with args()

# Check what arguments a function accepts
args(mean)
# function (x, trim = 0, na.rm = FALSE, ...)

# Now call correctly
mean(c(1, 2, NA), na.rm = TRUE) # Works

Fix 4: Remove extra arguments or wrap in …

# Wrong
my_func <- function(x) {
 return(x * 2)
}
my_func(5, extra = "hello") # Error: unused argument

# Correct
my_func <- function(x, ...) {
 return(x * 2)
}
my_func(5, extra = "hello") # Works, extra is ignored

Examples

# Example 1: Typo in argument
log(x = 10, basee = 10)
# Error in log(x = 10, basee = 10) : unused argument (basee = 10)

# Example 2: Wrong function argument
c(1, 2, 3, recursive = TRUE)
# Error in c(1, 2, 3, recursive = TRUE) : unused argument (recursive = TRUE)

# Example 3: Missing library
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(data = iris) + geom_point() + ggtitle("Plot")
# Works with ggplot2 loaded

# Example 4: Base R vs ggplot
plot(1:10, ggtitle = "My Plot")
# Error: unused argument (ggtitle = "My Plot")

Related Errors

  • wrong-number-args — missing required argument
  • object-not-found — undefined variable
  • error-in-plot — plot function errors

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 — Argument of type 'X' is not assignable to parameter
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable to parameter of type ‘Y’

TS2345 occurs when you pass an argument to a function that does not match the expected parameter type. This is one of the most common TypeScript errors and usually indicates a type mismatch at a call site.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Passing wrong type to function
function greet(name: string) {
 console.log(`Hello, ${name}`);
}
greet(42); // TS2345: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'

// Cause 2: Nullable argument to non-nullable parameter
function process(value: number) {
 return value * 2;
}
process(null); // TS2345 if strictNullChecks is on

// Cause 3: Array type mismatch
function logItems(items: string[]) {
 items.forEach(item => console.log(item));
}
logItems([1, 2, 3]); // TS2345

How to Fix

Fix 1: Pass the correct type

greet("Alice");

Fix 2: Handle null with union type

function process(value: number | null) {
 if (value === null) return 0;
 return value * 2;
}

Fix 3: Map array to correct type

logItems([1, 2, 3].map(String));

Fix 4: Use overloads for flexible signatures

function format(input: string): string;
function format(input: number): string;
function format(input: string | number): string {
 return String(input);
}
format("hello");
format(42);

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — assignment rather than call site.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — wrong argument count.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — no overload accepts the arguments.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 — Argument Type Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable

TS2345 occurs when you pass an argument to a function that doesn’t match the expected parameter type. This is one of the most common TypeScript errors and covers a wide range of type incompatibilities.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Wrong primitive type
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42); // TS2345: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'

// Cause 2: Missing required properties in object argument
function createUser(user: { name: string; age: number }) {}
createUser({ name: "Alice" }); // TS2345: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Array type mismatch
function processItems(items: number[]) {}
processItems(["a", "b"]); // TS2345: Type 'string[]' not assignable to 'number[]'

// Cause 4: Nullable argument to non-nullable parameter
function handle(input: string) {}
handle(null); // TS2345 if strictNullChecks is on

Solutions

Fix 1: Match the expected argument type

// Wrong
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42);

// Correct
greet("Alice");

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

// Wrong
function createUser(user: { name: string; age: number }) {}
createUser({ name: "Alice" });

// Correct
createUser({ name: "Alice", age: 30 });

Fix 3: Cast or convert arguments to the expected type

function processItems(items: number[]) {}
processItems(["1", "2"].map(Number)); // Convert strings to numbers

Fix 4: Use union types to accept multiple types

function handle(input: string | null) {
 if (input === null) return;
 // input is string here
}
handle(null); // OK

Examples

// Example 1: Incompatible object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
function startServer(config: Config) {}
startServer({ host: "localhost" }); // TS2345

// Example 2: Array vs single value
function log(items: string[]) {}
log("hello"); // TS2345: 'string' is not assignable to 'string[]'

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — assignment context rather than argument context.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — function overloads don’t match any signature.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments, but got Y — wrong argument count.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 v2 — Argument Type Mismatch Fix
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument Type Mismatch (v2)

This variant of TS2345 covers complex argument type mismatches including callback parameters, generic inference failures, and situations where the expected type is a complex mapped or conditional type.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2345: Argument of type 'string' is not assignable to parameter of type 'number'
  • TS2345: Argument of type '(x: number) => string' is not assignable to parameter of type '(x: number) => number'
  • TS2345: Argument of type 'User' is not assignable to parameter of type 'Partial<User>'
  • TS2345: Type '0' is not assignable to type '1'

This error fires when you call a function with arguments whose types don’t match the parameter types in the function signature.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2349 — This expression is not callable
item.description:
TS2349 — This expression is not callable

TS2349 occurs when you try to call an expression as a function, but its type does not include a call signature. The expression is not a function or callable object.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling a non-function variable
const name: string = "Alice";
name(); // TS2349: This expression is not callable

// Cause 2: Importing a module but calling the wrong thing
import config from "./config";
config(); // TS230 if config is an object, not a function

// Cause 3: Calling a class as a function
class Foo {}
Foo(); // TS2349 — must use 'new'

// Cause 4: Overloaded function not callable as expected
function create(): string;
function create(n: number): number[];
function create(n?: number): string | number[] {
 return n ? [] : "";
}
create; // TS2349 if accessed without calling

How to Fix

Fix 1: Verify the variable is a function

const greet = (name: string) => `Hello, ${name}`;
greet("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use ’new’ for class instantiation

const instance = new Foo();

Fix 3: Check import name

import { createConfig } from "./config";
createConfig(); // call the actual function

Related Errors

  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — calling a type as a value.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined identifier.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — argument count mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2349 — This Expression Is Not Callable
item.description:
TS2349 — This expression is not callable

TS2349 occurs when you try to call a value as a function using (), but its type doesn’t have a call signature. This happens when a variable holds a non-function value or when a type is not callable.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling a non-function value
const name = "Alice";
name(); // TS2349: This expression is not callable

// Cause 2: Calling an object as a function
const obj = { greet: "hello" };
obj(); // TS2349

// Cause 3: Calling a class instance as a function
class Greeter {
 greet() { return "hello"; }
}
const g = new Greeter();
g(); // TS2349

// Cause 4: Property that resolves to a non-function
interface Service {
 handler: string | (() => void);
}
function invoke(service: Service) {
 service.handler(); // TS2349: handler might be a string
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Verify the value is actually a function

// Wrong
const name = "Alice";
name();

// Correct
const greet = () => "Hello, Alice!";
greet();

Fix 2: Use a type guard before calling

interface Service {
 handler: string | (() => void);
}

function invoke(service: Service) {
 if (typeof service.handler === "function") {
 service.handler(); // OK
 }
}

Fix 3: Call the correct method

// Wrong
const obj = { greet: "hello" };
obj();

// Correct
const obj = { greet: () => "hello" };
obj.greet();

Fix 4: Ensure proper function assignment

// Wrong — property is not a function
const config = {
 init: false,
};
config.init(); // TS2349

// Correct
const config = {
 init: () => console.log("initialized"),
};
config.init();

Examples

// Example 1: Module default export is not a function
import myModule from "./my-module";
myModule(); // TS2349 if default export is an object

// Example 2: Array methods confused with call
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr(); // TS2349: arrays are not callable

Related Errors

  • TS2351: This expression is not constructable — trying to use new on non-constructable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — callable but wrong argument types.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2554 — Expected X arguments but got Y
item.description:
TS2554 — Expected X arguments but got Y

TS2554 occurs when you call a function with a different number of arguments than its signature expects. This can mean too many or too few arguments.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required arguments
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {
 return `${greeting}, ${name}`;
}
greet("Alice"); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1

// Cause 2: Too many arguments
function add(a: number, b: number) {
 return a + b;
}
add(1, 2, 3); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 3

// Cause 3: Calling constructor with wrong count
class Point {
 constructor(x: number, y: number) {}
}
new Point(1); // TS2554

How to Fix

Fix 1: Provide the correct number of arguments

greet("Alice", "Hello");

Fix 2: Make parameters optional

function greet(name: string, greeting: string = "Hello") {
 return `${greeting}, ${name}`;
}
greet("Alice"); // OK

Fix 3: Use rest parameters for variadic functions

function log(...args: string[]) {
 args.forEach(a => console.log(a));
}
log("a", "b", "c"); // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type mismatch.
  • TS2794: Expected 1 arguments but got 0 — zero-argument variant.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload resolution failure.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2769 — No overload matches this call
item.description:
TS2769 — No overload matches this call

TS2769 occurs when you call a function with overloaded signatures and none of the overloads match the provided arguments. TypeScript cannot find a single overload that accepts your argument types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Arguments don't match any overload
function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: number): number;
function process(input: string | number): string | number {
 return input;
}
process(true); // TS2769: no overload matches

// Cause 2: Spread arguments that don't fit
function log(msg: string): void;
function log(msg: string, level: number): void;
function log(...args: any[]): void {}
log("hello", "world"); // TS2769: 'string' not assignable to 'number'

// Cause 3: Generic constraint failure
function wrap<T extends HTMLElement>(el: T): T { return el; }
wrap(document.getElementById("myId")); // TS2769 if null

How to Fix

Fix 1: Match an existing overload

process("hello"); // matches first overload
process(42); // matches second overload

Fix 2: Add a new overload for your use case

function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: number): number;
function process(input: boolean): string; // add boolean overload
function process(input: string | number | boolean): string | number {
 return input;
}

Fix 3: Fix the argument type

const el = document.getElementById("myId");
if (el) {
 wrap(el); // el is now HTMLElement, not null
}

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — single-signature argument mismatch.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — wrong argument count.
  • TS2769: No overload matches — complex overload failure.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2769 v2 — No Overload Matches This Call Fix
item.description:
TS2769 — No Overload Matches This Call (v2)

This variant of TS2769 covers complex overload failures involving overloaded functions from libraries like React, Express, or DOM APIs where the combination of arguments doesn’t match any available signature.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2769: No overload matches this call. Overload 1 of 12 ...
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call. The last overload ...
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call. Expected 2 arguments, but got 3

A function has multiple call signatures (overloads). TypeScript checks each one and finds that none match the arguments you provided. The error lists all available overloads.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2794 — Expected 1 arguments but got 0
item.description:
TS2794 — Expected 1 arguments but got 0

TS2794 occurs when you call a function that requires arguments but provide none. In the context of incrementing, this often happens when trying to increment a value using a function that expects an argument.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Function requires an argument
function increment(value: number) {
 return value + 1;
}
increment(); // TS2794: Expected 1 arguments, but got 0

// Cause 2: Calling method without required parameter
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.indexOf(); // TS2794: Expected 1 arguments, but got 0

// Cause 3: Constructor expecting arguments
class Counter {
 constructor(initial: number) {}
}
new Counter(); // TS2794

How to Fix

Fix 1: Provide the required argument

increment(5);

Fix 2: Use optional parameters

function increment(value: number = 0) {
 return value + 1;
}
increment(); // OK — uses default

Fix 3: Use default parameter in constructor

class Counter {
 constructor(initial: number = 0) {}
}
new Counter(); // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2554: Expected X arguments but got Y — general argument count mismatch.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — argument type issues.
  • TS2488: Type must have Symbol.iterator method — iterator errors.
